title: Topper's Notes Strip
statement_md:
The branch topper has lent you their exam notes for one night. The notes are a single long line of lowercase letters `S` — no spaces, no punctuation, that's just how they write. The xerox shop near the hostel gate charges per letter, so you can afford to photocopy only **one continuous strip** of the notes.

A strip is useful only if it contains **every letter of the keyword** `P` the professor hinted at in the last class — counted with repeats. If `P` has two `a`s, your strip must contain at least two `a`s. The order of letters inside the strip does not matter.

For each test case, print the length of the **shortest** useful strip. If no strip of `S` works, print `0`.
input_format_md:
The first line contains a single integer `T` — the number of test cases.

For each test case:
- the first line contains the notes string `S`
- the second line contains the keyword `P`

Both strings contain only lowercase English letters `a`–`z`.
output_format_md: For each test case, print a single integer on its own line — the length of the shortest useful strip, or `0` if no strip contains all of `P`'s letters.
constraints_md:
- `1 ≤ T ≤ 10`
- `1 ≤ |P| ≤ |S| ≤ 2 × 10^4`
- the total length of all `S` strings in one input is at most `4 × 10^4`
- `S` and `P` contain only lowercase English letters

starter_code.python:
import sys

def solve_case(s, p):
    # your code here — return the length of the shortest useful strip, or 0
    return 0

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    t = int(data[0])
    pos = 1
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        s = data[pos]; p = data[pos + 1]; pos += 2
        out.append(str(solve_case(s, p)))
    print("\n".join(out))

main()

reference_solution.python:
import sys

def solve_case(s, p):
    need = [0] * 26
    for ch in p:
        need[ord(ch) - 97] += 1
    have = [0] * 26
    missing = len(p)
    n = len(s)
    best = n + 1
    left = 0
    for right in range(n):
        c = ord(s[right]) - 97
        have[c] += 1
        if have[c] <= need[c]:
            missing -= 1
        while missing == 0:
            if right - left + 1 < best:
                best = right - left + 1
            cl = ord(s[left]) - 97
            have[cl] -= 1
            if have[cl] < need[cl]:
                missing += 1
            left += 1
    return best if best <= n else 0

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    t = int(data[0])
    pos = 1
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        s = data[pos]; p = data[pos + 1]; pos += 2
        out.append(str(solve_case(s, p)))
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out) + "\n")

main()

hints: Fixing where the strip starts and re-scanning ahead every single time repeats a lot of work. As the start moves right, does the earliest useful end ever move left?

Keep 26 letter counts between two markers. Push the right marker until every keyword letter is covered (with repeats), then pull the left marker in while coverage still holds, recording the length each time. Each marker only moves forward, so the whole pass is linear.
